text: The surgeon ensures that the cut edge of the ileum mesentery is straight, preparing for the next step in the intracorporeal anastomosis during the robotic right colectomy.
The surgeon ensures the cut edge of the ileum mesentery is straight, preparing for the intracorporeal anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the straight cut edge of the ileum mesentery, ensuring that the anastomosis will not have a twist.
The video shows the cut edge of the ilium mesentery, which appears straight, ensuring proper alignment for the anastomosis.
The ilium is positioned in a gentle C shape, aligning closely with the transverse colon, preparing for anastomosis.
The ilium is positioned to form a gentle C shape, aligning closely with the transverse colon, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon uses the robotic third arm to provide counter-attraction on the bowel, facilitating the creation of the enterotomy.
The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to apply counter-attraction on the bowel, stabilizing it for further manipulation during the intracorporeal anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon prepares for the enterotomy, ensuring the cut edge of the ilium mesentery is straight to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to make an enterotomy on the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel, ensuring the incision is positioned to prevent the bowel from rolling in on itself during stapling.
The surgeon prepares to make an enterotomy, positioning the surgical instruments away from the previously indicated area.
The surgeon positions the bowel on the anti-mesenteric side, preparing for stapling, which will cause the bowel to roll in on itself.
The surgeon uses cautery to enter the bowel, ensuring the incision is at least two centimeters away from the staple line. The bowel is lifted to confirm entry into the lumen before cutting.
The surgeon ensures that the enterotomy is made at least two centimeters away from the staple line on the bowel, maintaining a safe distance for the procedure.
The surgeon uses cautery to make an incision on the bowel, with the third arm providing counter-attraction to stabilize the tissue.
The surgeon lifts the bowel and makes a precise cut once inside the lumen, ensuring accurate entry into the bowel's interior.
The surgeon ensures a full-thickness bite of the intestinal wall to achieve a precise enterotomy.
The surgeon ensures a full-thickness bite of the intestinal wall, preparing for a precise aneurotomy.
The surgeon uses the third arm to provide counter traction on the transverse colon, repeating the process previously applied to the ileum.

The surgeon repeats the process of entering the transverse colon with cautery, ensuring the incision is made on the anti-mesenteric side and maintaining a distance of at least two centimeters from the staple line.
The surgeon utilizes the third robotic arm to provide counter traction, stabilizing the tissue for precise manipulation during the procedure.

The surgeon opens the colon using cautery, avoiding the tinea coli to ensure the incision is positioned more anti-mesenterically. The colon is opened sharply, similar to the previous technique used on the bowel.
The surgeon opens the bowel, avoiding the tinea coli, as the incision is made in a position slightly farther from the anti-mesenteric side.
The surgeon opens the colon using sharp dissection and cautery, similar to the technique previously used on the bowel.
The surgeon prepares to make an incision on the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel using the cutting setting on the electrocautery device, with the option to use coagulation mode if preferred.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut through the bowel wall, positioning one blade inside and one blade outside the lumen, lifting the tissue for precise cutting.
The surgeon uses scissors with one blade inside and one blade outside the bowel wall, lifting the tissue and cutting through it.
The surgeon inserts the stapler into the ileum, maneuvering the mobile ileum towards the relatively immobile transverse colon to prepare for anastomosis.
The surgeon inserts the stapler into the ileum, preparing to secure the tissue.
The surgeon maneuvers the relatively mobile ileum towards the relatively immobile transverse colon, preparing for anastomosis between these two sections of the bowel.
The surgeon demonstrates the ease of maneuvering the free ilium with a closed stapler, positioning it relative to the more immobile transverse colon.
The surgeon demonstrates the ease of repositioning the ilium using a closed stapler, maneuvering it to the desired location relative to the transverse colon.
The ilium is visibly free and mobile, allowing for manipulation and positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the ease of moving the ilium compared to the relatively immobile transverse colon, highlighting the ilium's mobility during positioning.
The surgeon positions the ilium and aligns the stapler with the colon, ensuring precise alignment before firing the stapler to create an anastomosis.
The surgeon positions the ilium and then brings the stapler into the transverse colon, achieving precise alignment between the two structures.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the junction of the ilium and transverse colon, ensuring alignment before firing the stapler to secure the connection.

As the stapler opens, a third arm retracts the apex of the staple line upwards, aligning the edges of the staple line. The common channel is visible, and the staple line shows no bleeding, with the crotch of the staple line widely exposed.
The surgeon uses a third robotic arm to retract the apex of the staple line upwards as the stapler opens, aligning the edges of the staple line for precise positioning.
The camera view shows the interior of the common channel, with a clear view of the staple line, which is intact and not bleeding.
The crotch of the staple line is widely exposed, providing a clear view of the surgical area.
The surgeon focuses on closing the bottom crotch of the staple line meticulously to prevent leaks, ensuring the closure is secure and well-aligned.
The surgeon focuses on closing the bottom of the staple line, ensuring it is sealed properly to prevent leaks, using precise alignment and closure techniques.

The surgeon uses a V-lock PDS suture on a V-twin needle to close the bottom crotch of the staple line, taking smaller bites for precision. The needle tip falls off due to a defect, prompting the use of a new needle and suture.
The surgeon uses a V-lock PDS suture on a V-twin needle, running the twelve-inch suture back over itself to assist in closing the surgical site.
The surgeon takes smaller bites with the suture at the bottom crotch of the staple line, ensuring precise closure to prevent leaks.
The surgeon encounters a defect in the V-lock suture, causing the needle tip to detach unexpectedly during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a new needle and suture to continue the closure process, ensuring proper alignment and tension in the surgical site.

The surgeon uses a third arm to retract the apex of the staple line, aligning the edges of the bowel precisely. Full thickness bites are taken along the suture line, and as the surgeon approaches the apex, the third arm is removed to retract the bowel posteriorly, exposing the entire suture line clearly.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to retract the apex of the staple line, aligning the edges of the bowel neatly.
The surgeon takes full thickness bites with a suture, running along the bowel, aligning the edges smoothly.
The surgeon approaches the apex of the bowel, using the third robotic arm to retract the bowel for better alignment and visibility.
The surgeon positions the instruments posterior to the apex, preparing to manipulate the bowel and suture line.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to retract the bowel, exposing the apex of the staple line clearly for further manipulation.
The entire suture line is clearly visible, showing well-aligned full thickness bites along the tissue.
The surgeon takes large bites with the suture needle, rolling the last portion of the bowel tip inward to complete the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon takes large, full-thickness bites with the suture needle, carefully rolling the last portion of the tissue tip inward.
The surgeon uses the robotic arm to roll the tissue with a firm bite, completing the closure of the suture line near the apex.

The surgeon runs a suture from the apex downwards, taking robust seromuscular bites on both sides of the bowel wall along the suture line. This technique imbricates the suture line, effectively rolling it over itself to provide an additional protective layer.
The surgeon runs the suture from the apex downward, taking robust seromuscular bites of the bowel wall on both sides of the exposed suture line.
The surgeon imbricates the bowel wall using a pseudo-limber type suture technique, folding the tissue over the suture line to reinforce the closure.
The surgeon manipulates the suture line, rolling it over itself to enhance closure and stability of the bowel wall.

The surgeon uses a long, twelve-inch relock suture to continue securing the bowel edges, which are visibly rolling in on themselves. The suture line progresses down to the previously closed bottom crotch, with thick, full-thickness bites ensuring a secure closure.
The surgeon uses a long, twelve-inch relock instrument to manage the extra suture left over from the previous closure, ensuring efficient use of the remaining material.
The edges of the bowel are visibly rolling in on themselves, creating a smooth, imbricated suture line.
The surgeon continues suturing down to the previously closed bottom crotch, using thick, full-thickness bites to roll the suture line together.

The surgeon places a crotch stitch at the point of maximal tension on the staple line to prevent unrolling, taking a thick bite at the edge of the anastomosis to achieve complete closure. The completed anastomosis is visible.
The surgeon is positioned at the very edge of the anastomosis, focusing on the precise alignment and closure of the bowel edges.
The surgeon visually identifies the crotch of the staple line, located directly below the surgical instruments.
The surgeon takes a thick bite of tissue at the staple line, resulting in a complete closure of the surgical site.
The completed anastomosis is visible, showing a well-aligned and secure connection between the two sections of tissue.
